title: A practical guide explains how models learn low-, medium- and high-effort reasoning
friendlyTitle: How AI models learn to think for shorter or longer
summary: Sebastian Raschka explains how reasoning models can be trained to produce different effort levels, from short low-cost traces to longer high-effort work. The article connects supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards and token-budget controls to the reasoning settings now common in model APIs.
friendlySummary: This guide explains how developers train reasoning models to use different amounts of effort. A model can give a quick answer for an easy task or spend more tokens on a harder one.
whyItMatters: Reasoning effort is a cost-and-latency control, not simply a quality switch. Developers can reserve higher settings for difficult or verifiable tasks, then evaluate whether extra tokens actually improve outcomes instead of applying maximum effort everywhere.
friendlyWhyItMatters: More thinking costs more time and money, and it does not always help. Use higher effort for difficult tasks and measure whether the result really improves.
